skill.name: tester
skill.description: Testing workflow skill for the GabeDA React and Django application. It helps design test strategies, create pytest and Playwright tests, execute local test suites, analyze failures, and write test reports.
skill.summary: Design, run, and report full-stack GabeDA tests with pytest and Playwright.
skill.author: Brownbull

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, environment, and sensitive-data patterns, but review found they are mostly markdown testing examples for pytest, npm, Playwright, localhost APIs, and test fixtures. The main confirmed risk is legitimate but broad testing guidance that can write files and run project commands, so publication is acceptable with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Project Test Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run npm, Playwright, and pytest commands in the target project. This is expected for a testing skill, but project scripts can execute arbitrary local code if the repository is untrusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically part of test execution. The risk depends on the target repository scripts, so it is a legitimate medium-risk capability rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Writes for Tests and Reports
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to create test files, strategies, reports, and findings in project folders. This is necessary for the workflow, but it can modify the user workspace and should be constrained to intended paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write locations are documented clearly and match the skill purpose. There is no hidden persistence, but workspace modification is a real operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Browser Token Storage Test Examples
medium_findings.description: The examples read access and refresh tokens from localStorage to assert login behavior. This is a common testing pattern, but users should avoid applying it to real accounts or exposing token values in reports.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code reads browser tokens, but only for local test assertions and does not send them elsewhere. The risk is moderate because copied tests could leak secrets if used against real environments.

low_findings.title: Localhost and Mock Network Examples
low_findings.description: The network findings point to localhost Playwright navigation, localhost API setup, and a mocked external API example. No evidence found of unauthorized exfiltration or remote command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed network examples are local development URLs or mocked requests. This strongly supports a false-positive classification for malicious network activity.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access Limited to CI Settings
low_findings.description: The environment variable findings are Playwright configuration checks for CI behavior. No evidence found that secrets are read or transmitted.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only environment variable observed is process.env.CI in configuration. This is a standard test-runner pattern with no credential access.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive-Data False Positives
low_findings.description: The browser credential and database findings map to cookie cleanup and in-memory SQLite test configuration, not credential theft or sensitive database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line context shows cleanup and test database setup. These are benign testing patterns, though users should still avoid real credentials in tests.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Scanner Noise
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography detections appear to be keyword collisions in testing documentation, such as smoke tests and pytest slow markers. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, or unsafe cryptographic implementation intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged context is test taxonomy and pytest marker text. I did not find semantic evidence of weak cryptographic code in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Test Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Markdown examples include shell commands for npm and pytest test execution. These should be run only after reviewing the local repository scripts and dependencies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are direct and intentional. They are safe in a trusted project, but externally supplied scripts can execute arbitrary code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Token Access in Browser Context
dangerous_patterns.description: A Playwright example reads tokens from localStorage as assertions. This should remain limited to disposable test accounts and local environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern directly accesses token values but does not exfiltrate them. The concern is misuse or accidental logging in adapted tests.

content.user_title: Build Full-Stack Test Coverage for GabeDA
content.value_statement: Full-stack teams need repeatable tests across React, Django, APIs, and user workflows. This skill provides structured test design, execution guidance, and reporting patterns for GabeDA.
actual_capabilities: Designs unit, integration, E2E, smoke, and UAT test strategies for GabeDA features.
Provides pytest patterns for Django models, serializers, API endpoints, fixtures, and database tests.
Provides Playwright patterns for browser flows, authentication fixtures, locators, and test data setup.
Lists local commands for running frontend, backend, coverage, and targeted test suites.
Defines report formats for failures, coverage gaps, recommendations, and release readiness.
limitations: The guidance is tailored to the GabeDA project structure and may need adaptation for other applications.
It references local test credentials and localhost services that must be replaced in production-like environments.
It does not provide a standalone test runner or enforce sandboxing by itself.
It depends on existing project dependencies such as pytest, Django REST Framework, Playwright, and npm scripts.

use_cases.title: Plan Feature Test Coverage
use_cases.description: Create a focused test plan for a new GabeDA feature with happy paths, edge cases, and acceptance criteria.

use_cases.title: Add Backend Regression Tests
use_cases.description: Write pytest coverage for models, serializers, API endpoints, database behavior, and authentication rules.

use_cases.title: Validate Release Readiness
use_cases.description: Run smoke and E2E checks, collect failures, and produce a concise report for deployment decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Smoke Test Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GabeDA smoke test checklist for login, dashboard access, company creation, and file upload. Include expected results and report sections.

prompt_templates.title: Write Backend Pytest Cases
prompt_templates.prompt: Design pytest cases for the company creation API. Cover authenticated success, duplicate RUT validation, unauthenticated access, and membership creation.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Playwright E2E Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Playwright E2E test plan for a user who logs in, creates two companies, switches between them, and confirms dashboard updates.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Release Test Run
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these frontend and backend test results. Summarize failures, likely causes, coverage gaps, release risk, and recommended next actions.

output_examples.input: Design UAT coverage for CSV upload.
output: A UAT scenario list covering valid files, invalid formats, large uploads, missing columns, and permission errors.
Acceptance criteria for each scenario, including expected messages and data visibility.
A report outline for stakeholder review.
A concise execution plan for frontend and backend smoke checks.
A pass, fail, or blocked summary with failing areas grouped by feature.
Deployment guidance based on critical path status.

output_examples.input: Create backend tests for company statistics.
output: A pytest scenario matrix for authenticated, unauthenticated, empty data, and populated data cases.
Fixture recommendations for users, companies, memberships, and uploads.
Assertions focused on status codes, response fields, and access control.

best_practices: Run generated commands only inside the intended project workspace and review package scripts first.
Use isolated test users, fixtures, and local services instead of production data or production credentials.
Store reports and findings in the documented testing folders with clear dates and test types.
anti_patterns: Do not run project test commands from an untrusted repository without reviewing scripts and dependencies.
Do not reuse the example passwords or bearer token placeholders in shared or production environments.
Do not treat localStorage token assertions as permission to extract or expose real user tokens.

faq.question: What project is this skill designed for?
faq.answer: It is designed for the GabeDA application, which uses a React frontend and Django backend.

faq.question: Does the skill execute tests automatically?
faq.answer: It provides commands and workflows that an agent may run when the user authorizes local test execution.

faq.question: Which test frameworks does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers pytest for Django backend tests and Playwright for browser-based frontend tests.

faq.question: Can it generate release reports?
faq.answer: Yes. It defines report formats for test totals, failures, coverage gaps, and recommendations.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with production credentials?
faq.answer: No. Use isolated test accounts, local services, and non-production data when following this guidance.

faq.question: Can teams adapt it outside GabeDA?
faq.answer: Yes, but paths, commands, fixtures, and application-specific scenarios must be changed.
